get.description:
This endpoint can be use to retrieve all the event search jobs created
by the community of the user.

One can filter jobs given
* their author (`match[created_by]`),
* their status (`match[status]`),
* their term (`match[term]`)

Jobs can be sorted given
* the date of creation (`sort=created_at`),
* the end of execution (`sort=ended_at`),
* the start of execution (`sort=started_at`),
* the ttl of their results (`sort=results_ttl`),

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)
 - “View events” (`1cbb91c7-e380-4d9a-98d1-6c850fda8e98`)

post.description:
Earliest time and latest time denote the UTC time boundaries of the search.
Latest time must be after the earliest time.

They accept both an absolute time or a relative time.
The absolute time must follow the ISO 8601 format such as:
* `2011-11-04`
* `2011-11-04T00:05:23`
* `2011-11-04 00:05:23.283`

Please note that microsecond precision will be ignored (digits beyond the third decimal place).

The relative time format denotes a delta with the task execution time.
It follows the format `(\+|-)(\d+)(S|M|H|D|W)` with case ignored
and accepts seconds (s), minutes (m), hours (h), days (d) and weeks (w).
Below are valid relative times:
* `+3d`: three days in the future
* `-2w`: two weeks before now
* `-7d`: seven days before now
* `now`: current time

When a new search job is executed, the job is retained in the system for
a period of time, called the `results_ttl`. The job automaticaly expires
when the `results_ttl` limit is reached. After a job expires,
all its artifacts are deleted.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)
 - “View events” (`1cbb91c7-e380-4d9a-98d1-6c850fda8e98`)

post.summary: Export all the events matching a search job
post.description:
Unlike the GET endpoint, which immediately returns a downloadable file containing the exported events,
this POST endpoint initiates an asynchronous export task for large datasets. This is suitable for cases
where the export may take significant time or exceed size limits for direct downloads.

One can use the `fields` parameter to specify a subset of fields to include in the export.
By default, only the `@timestamp` and `message` fields are exported if no specific fields are provided.
For example, to only retrieve the original message:
```
{
    "fields": ["message"]
}
```

You can provide custom S3 settings by including the `s3` parameter in the request body.
These settings will override the default S3 settings for the export.
For example, to use your own S3 bucket, you can provide the following settings:
```
{
    "s3": {
        "access_key_id": "custom_access_key",
        "secret_access_key": "custom_secret_key",
        "endpoint_url": "https://custom-s3-endpoint.com",
        "region_name": "custom-region",
        "bucket_name": "custom-bucket",
        "sse_customer_algorithm": "AES256",
        "sse_customer_key": "<base64-encoded-key>",
        "sse_customer_key_md5": "<base64-md5>"
    }
}
```
The following prerequisites must be met for using custom S3 settings:
- The S3 bucket must be created and accessible.
- The provided credentials must have the necessary permissions to write
to the specified S3 bucket and generate pre-signed URLs.
- The endpoint URL must be valid and reachable.
- The following S3 features must be supported:
  - `CreateMultipartUpload`
  - `UploadPart`
  - `AbortMultiPartUpload`
  - `CompleteMultipartUpload`
  - `UploadPartCopy`
  - `DeleteObjects`
  - `GeneratePresignedUrl`

The response contains a `task_uuid` that can be used to track the progress and retrieve the exported file
once the task is completed.

Use this endpoint when you expect a large number of events.

To track the status of the export task, you can use the `task_uuid` returned in the response.
One can use the endpoint `/v1/tasks/<task_uuid>` to monitor the progress of the export
and get the download url of the result once the task is completed.

Because the exported file can be stored in an S3 bucket with server-side encryption
using customer-provided keys (SSE-C), specific headers must be specified when downloading the file.
Here is an example of how to download the exported file using `curl` with SSE-C:
curl "<PRESIGNED_URL>"         -H "x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-algorithm: AES256"         -H "x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key: <base64 key>"         -H "x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key-MD5: <base64 MD5>"         -o export.json.gz

The following permissions are required:
 - “Massive export of events” (`d146917d-2623-436b-8fcd-5467510cfeca`)

post.description:
This endpoint can be used if you need to make a _fresh_ token for a user. A fresh token requires the user to
send its credentials (email address, password and MFA tokens).

Unlike standard login endpoint, it only returns a new “access token”. No “refresh token” is generated.

get.summary: Return all public keys used by `communityapi` to sign JWT tokens
get.description:
All configured keys are exported in this endpoint, at this time, we only support _one_ EdDSA key and one RSA
key.

Returns:
  A JWKS (JSON Web Key Set).

post.description:
This endpoint allows to authenticate a user given its email address (identifier) and its password.

This endpoint pushes two authentication cookies:

- _Access token_, which has a short life cookie that is usable on other SEKOIA.IO endpoints,
- _Refresh token_, which is a long life cookie that is usable only on SEKOIA.IO’s authentication API to request
  a renewed access token (via the `/auth/refresh` endpoint).

If the user that wants to log-in has enabled multi-factor authentication (MFA), then, this endpoint returns a
401 error (with the `MFA102` error code) requesting the user to complete the MFA challenge.

In order to complete the MFA challenge, the right `mfa_data` should be submitted.

This endpoint creates a new “access token” that is pushed to the end user via a cookie.

This endpoint requires to have a valid “refresh token” (submitted JWT token must have been created by the
authentication API and shouldn’t be expired).

This endpoint revokes the current JWT tokens (either “refresh token” and “access token”).

If user isn’t authenticated yet, an `AU101` error is raised.

post.summary: Switch current user to another community
post.description:
This endpoint allows a user to switch to another community he/she belongs to. To proceed, you have to provide
the avatar (representation of the current user in the requested community) identifier.

This endpoint creates a brand new “access token” and “refresh token” that are pushed as cookies (more
information in the `/auth/login` endpoint).

get.description:
This endpoint checks if the submitted JWT access token was generated by SEKOIA.IO’s authentication API and is
not expired or revoked.

JWT token is retrieved either from the cookies in the request or from the `Authorization` HTTP header.

If valid, this endpoint returns the clear text representation of the token, including the `user_claims` data.

post.summary: Register a beneficiary for a list of licenses in the community
Create, update, or schedule a PAYG license for the selected community
post.description:
Expected behavior:
1. If there is no conflicting active license at requested dates, PAYG is created
   at requested dates.
2. If an active PAYG/TRIAL overlaps requested dates, it is ended right before
   the new PAYG starts. For immediate replacements (start <= today), the new
   PAYG starts tomorrow.
3. If a valid non-PAYG license is active, PAYG is scheduled right after it ends.
4. If a PAYG is already scheduled in the future for the same module, that
   scheduled PAYG is updated.

As a result, users can temporarily see two PAYG licenses while transitioning:
one ending before the next one starts.

400.description: Expired PAYG license cannot be terminated

delete.summary: Terminate a PAYG license for the selected community
delete.description:
Behavior depends on the target license dates:
- Active PAYG: terminated by setting end date to today (not hard-deleted).
- Future/scheduled PAYG: deleted from licenses, because it has not started yet.
- Expired PAYG: rejected with 400.

This endpoint only applies to PAYG licenses.

post.description:
User that has lost his password can ask to receive a link to
reset its password even if the current password is not
known. When a user calls this endpoint, it receives a message
on his e-mail address with a link that will point to the
`reset_password` endpoint.

This endpoint must be called with a valid e-mail
address. Account associated to that e-mail should already
exist, be confirmed and not deleted. Last reset password
request should have been called more than 5 minutes ago.

If user has already enabled the multi-factor authentication,
then the current token data is required to change its password
(see the `reset_password` endpoint).

This endpoint allows a user to change its current
password. To proceed, the current password and the new
password are asked.
The new password must respect a certain complexity in order to be accepted.

requestBody.description: The current password associated to the account

post.summary: Define a new password after a reset claim
post.description:
After the user asked for a password reset using the
`/forget_password` endpoint, it receives a link which contains
a token that can be used to call this endpoint.

In order to define a new password, user should call this
endpoint with a valid reset token (received by e-mail) and
its new password. If user has enabled multi-factor
authentication, then the current token data is required to
change its password.

requestBody.description: The temporary token for the password reset

get.description:
Datasource identify a source of data. It's defined by the MITRE ATT&CK framework.
This paginated endpoint list all datasources. A set of filters can be
specified to focus on certain datasources.

One can filter datasources given
* the identifier of the datasource (`match[uuid]`),
* the name of the datasource (`match[name]`)

A filter parameter defines filtering on the specified `field`.
A filter parameter can be specified any number of times, where each filter instance specifies
an additional filter to be applied to the resulting data. Said another way,
all filter fields are ANDed together. Each `field` MUST NOT occur more than once in a request.

It should be noted that each `match[]` MAY contain one or more values. Multiple values are separated by a comma
(U+002C COMMA, “,”) without any spaces. If multiple values are present, the match is treated as a logical OR.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)

parameters.description: limit a number of items (allowed range is 1-100), default is 20.

parameters.description: a number of items to skip, default is 0.

parameters.name: match[name]
parameters.description: Match datasources by their name

parameters.name: match[uuid]
parameters.description: Match datasources by their uuid

get.description:
This paginated endpoint can be used to retrieve the supported formats. A set of filters can be
specified to focus on certain formats.

One can filter intake formats given
* the name of the format (`match[name]`),
* the uuid of the format (`match[uuid]`),
* the uuid of the module (`match[module_uuid]`),
* uuid of the community  (`match[community_uuid]`),
* uuid of the datasource (`match[datasource_uuid]`),
* name of the datasouce  (`match[datasource_name]`).
* uuid of the automation module (`match[automation_module_uuid]`), is an alias for the module_uuid.
* uuid of the automation connector (`match[automation_connector_uuid]`).


One can list only intakes updated after a certain date
by using the `updated_after` filter

One can also add a “search” term that will search in multiple
places for that term.

The `name` filter accepts joker denoted by char '*'.

A filter parameter defines filtering on the specified `field`.
A filter parameter can be specified any number of times, where each filter instance specifies
an additional filter to be applied to the resulting data. Said another way,
all filter fields are ANDed together. Each `field` MUST NOT occur more than once in a request.

It should be noted that each `match[]` MAY contain one or more values. Multiple values are separated by a comma
(U+002C COMMA, “,”) without any spaces. If multiple values are present, the match is treated as a logical OR.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)
 - “View events” (`1cbb91c7-e380-4d9a-98d1-6c850fda8e98`)

get.summary: Get the available fields leveraged by the formats
get.description:
One can filter fields given

- the name of the field (`match[name]`)
- the type of the field (`match[type]`)
- the identifier of the module (`match[module_uuid]`)

A filter parameter defines filtering on the specified field. A filter parameter can be specified any number of
times, where each filter instance specifies an additional filter to be applied to the resulting data.
Said another way, all filter fields are ANDed together. Each field MUST NOT occur more than once in a request.

It should be noted that each `match[]` MAY contain one or more values. Multiple values are separated by a comma
(U+002C COMMA, “,”) without any spaces. If multiple values are present, the match is treated as a logical OR.

The `name` filter accepts joker denoted by char '*'.
The `module_uuid` filter will append custom fields, belonged to the module, to the response.

If valued to `true`, the parameter `all_fields` will append all custom fields to the response.

By default, eternal fields are returned. They can be excluded with `include_eternal` valued to `false`.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)
 - “View events” (`1cbb91c7-e380-4d9a-98d1-6c850fda8e98`)

parameters.description: Match fields by their type (separated by commas).

parameters.name: all_fields
parameters.description: Whether we should return all the fields or only the built-in.

parameters.name: include_eternal

get.description:
This endpoint returns the definition of the intake format.

This definition contains:
- the identifier of the format
- the name of the format
- the slug of the format
- the description of the format
- the parser that describe the steps to extract data
- the taxonomy of the parser
- the datasources of the logs
- the identifier of the module the intake format belongs to
- the identifier of the community the intake format belongs to

If the identifier of the community of an intake format is null, this format is not related to a community and
is considered as public.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)
 - “View events” (`1cbb91c7-e380-4d9a-98d1-6c850fda8e98`)

put.description:
This endpoint allows to update the definition of an intake format.
Only intake format related to a community can be updated. Public ones are immutables.

The following parameters can be update:
- the name
- the slug
- the description
- the parser
- the taxonomy
- the datasources

The modification of an intake format generate a revision. This revision will store the previous state of
the format. The revisions will shape the history of the format and allow to compare its evolution.

The following permissions are required:
 - “Manage intakes” (`efde546e-06db-4355-b2e5-84de1f66511b`)
 - “Manage intake formats” (`094827fb-7858-446d-bb7f-d041f60a8de7`)

delete.description:
This endpoint allows to delete an intake format
Only intake format related to a community can be deleted. Public ones are immutables.

Be careful when deleting an intake format, it will impact the intakes using it and invalidate new logs (no more
detection on them).

The following permissions are required:
 - “Manage intakes” (`efde546e-06db-4355-b2e5-84de1f66511b`)
 - “Manage intake formats” (`094827fb-7858-446d-bb7f-d041f60a8de7`)

set up its appliances to forward logs and to understand the purpose of the format.

The documentation is only accessible if the user can access the intake format.

The following permissions are required:
 - “View intakes” (`2f0da527-2cf1-4a00-8f4c-033bfb43ddeb`)

get.description:
The cyber kill chain (developed by Lockheed Martin) is an industry-accepted framework to
 describe and model the stages of an offensive cyber activity. An effective understanding of
the cyber kill chain greatly assists the information security professional in
 establishing the adversary’s tactics, techniques and procedures (TTPs).

The cyber kill chain is denoted by 7 steps:
1. Reconnaissance: Intruder selects target, researches it, and attempts to identify
 vulnerabilities in the target network.
2. Weaponization: Intruder creates remote access malware weapon, such as a virus or
 worm, tailored to one or more vulnerabilities.
3. Delivery: Intruder transmits weapon to target (e.g., via e-mail attachments,
 websites or USB drives).
4. Exploitation: Malware weapon's program code triggers, which takes action
 on target network to exploit vulnerability.
5. Installation: Malware weapon installs access point (e.g., "backdoor") usable by intruder.
6. Command and Control: Malware enables intruder to have "hands on the
 keyboard" persistent access to target network.
7. Actions on Objective: Intruder takes action to achieve their goals,
 such as data exfiltration, data destruction, or encryption for ransom.

This endpoint can be use to retrieve the different stages of the Cyber Kill Chain.

A stage is denoted by its unique identifier (`uuid`), its `name` and `description`.
In addition, every stage can be refered to by its `short_id`.

For example, the following denotes the **Reconnaissance** stage:
```
{
    'uuid': '73708d4f-419f-44aa-b505-288b522f9c30',
    'name': 'Reconnaissance',
    'description': 'Intruder selects target, researches it, and attempts to identify vulnerabilities.',
    'short_id': 'KCXKNfnJupq5'
}
```

The following permissions are required:
 - “View alerts” (`9ea2b8a3-593f-4bab-92f5-d0af9b563f6f`)

countermeasures.description: Response actions (countermeasures) recommended or applied in reaction to the alert. Each countermeasure has a name, a description, a list of concrete action steps to execute, and a lifecycle status (pending → activated or denied). They can originate from three sources, reflected in the `type` field: `text` (free-form analyst note, e.g. 'Isolate the affected host'), `intelligence_center` (a course of action sourced from SEKOIA.IO's threat intelligence, e.g. 'Block IOC X on your firewall'), or `openc2` (a machine-readable OpenC2 command sent to a security actuator). Included by default; opt out with `?countermeasures=false`.

tags.name: formats
tags.description:
Intakes Formats endpoint

This namespace allows to manage intake formats.

An intake format denotes the format implemented by the intake to analyze events.
It brings a parser, datasources, a taxonomy, smartdescriptions and a documentation.


The parser describes how data are extracted from the logs. It's compounded of a list of steps.
Each step will transform part of the event in a structured information and append this information in the final event.
This event follows the [Elastic Common Schema](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/ecs/index.html) specification to hold
extracted data.


However, if no ECS field can represent an extracted information, a list of custom fields can be defined to carry this
information. In this case, these fields are described in the taxonomy.


This structured information is understandable for machine, less for human. To ease it reading, smartdescriptions
describe how to display the event in the comprehensible way for a human. They will expose the relevant information of
the event and will aid in your daily work.


In order to select the best intake format to use, intake formats carry a list of datasources. These datasources
identify the sources of the logs and are defined by the MITRE ATT&CK framework.

To set up the logs forwarding and to use the intake format, the documentation will guide you. It will draw the process
to follow to enjoy your events on SEKOIA.IO.
